audit_status.source_base: Greek 1 Corinthians text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for apostolic rights, voluntary self-limitation, becoming all things, race/discipline imagery.

verses.restored: Am I not free? Am I not an apostle? Have I not seen Jesus our Lord? Are you not my work in the Lord?
If to others I am not an apostle, at least I am to you, for you are the seal of my apostleship in the Lord.
My defense to those examining me is this:
Do we not have authority to eat and drink?
Do we not have authority to take along a believing wife/sister, as also the rest of the apostles and the brothers of the Lord and Cephas?
Or only I and Barnabas, do we not have authority to refrain from working?
Who serves as soldier at one's own expense? Who plants a vineyard and does not eat its fruit? Or who shepherds a flock and does not eat from the milk of the flock?
Do I speak these things according to human reasoning? Or does not the Law also say these things?
For in the Law of Moses it is written: You shall not muzzle an ox treading grain. Is the Divine concerned for oxen only?
Or does he speak entirely because of us? Because of us it was written, that the one plowing ought to plow in hope, and the one threshing in hope of sharing.
If we sowed spiritual things among you, is it a great thing if we harvest your material things?
If others share this authority over you, do we not more? But we did not use this authority; rather, we endure all things so that we may give no hindrance to the good news of Messiah.
Do you not know that those working the sacred things eat from the temple, and those attending the altar share with the altar?
So also the Lord directed those announcing the good news to live from the good news.
But I have used none of these things. I do not write these things so that it may be done thus in me; for it would be beautiful/good for me rather to die than that anyone empty my boast.
For if I announce good news, there is no boast for me; necessity lies upon me, for woe is me if I do not announce good news.
For if I do this willingly, I have a wage; but if unwillingly, I have been entrusted with a stewardship.
What then is my wage? That announcing good news, I may make the good news without charge, so as not to use fully my authority in the good news.
For being free from all, I enslaved myself to all, so that I might gain the more.
To Judeans I became as Judean, so that I might gain Judeans; to those under Law as under Law, though not myself being under Law, so that I might gain those under Law.
To those without Law as without Law, not being without Divine law but within Messiah's law, so that I might gain those without Law.
To the weak I became weak, so that I might gain the weak. To all I have become all things, so that by all means I might save/heal some.
I do all things because of the good news, so that I may become a sharer with it.
Do you not know that those running in a stadium all run, but one receives the prize? Run in such a way that you may take hold.
Everyone competing exercises self-control in all things; they do it to receive a perishable crown, but we an imperishable.
Therefore I run this way, not uncertainly; I box this way, not beating air.
But I discipline my body and make it a servant, lest somehow, after proclaiming to others, I myself should become disapproved.

translation_notes: Paul's freedom is expressed through voluntary self-limitation for the good news, not through domination.
Athletic imagery is discipline for service, not body-hatred.
